title: Social Class
summary: Social class is an attempt to explain why inequality persists across generations in societies that claim to offer equal opportunity. Whether it does explain this — and which of several competing frameworks explains it best — is one of the most contested questions in social science.
hook: A child born to a surgeon is far more likely to become a surgeon than a child born to a cleaner. This is true in almost every society that has been studied. The question is not whether this happens — it clearly does — but why. Class analysis is the attempt to answer that question. The different frameworks give very different answers, and those answers have very different political implications.

beginner.theConcept:
Researchers have studied dozens of countries and found the same pattern everywhere. Children tend to end up in the same kind of work as their parents. The gap between families at the top and families at the bottom is not explained by talent alone. Social class is the name for the system of groups that shapes these patterns.

Class divides people by their position in the economic and social world. Some frameworks focus on what a family owns. Others look at what kind of work people do. Others look at the respect and social standing that comes with different jobs. All of these approaches share one key claim: where you end up in life is closely tied to where you started.
beginner.problemItSolves:
The puzzle that class analysis tries to solve is called persistence. Persistence means that inequality passes from parents to children, even in societies that believe everyone has a fair shot.

Studies show that children from wealthy families tend to do better in school. They attend better universities. They earn more as adults. This is true even when those children are not more talented than children from poorer families. Something beyond individual ability is at work. Class analysis tries to name and explain that something.

Without a concept like class, we are left with one explanation for much of what we see: people end up where they deserve to be. Class analysis challenges that story by pointing to the real advantages and disadvantages that come with the family you are born into.
beginner.howItWorks:
Several frameworks have shaped how people think about class. Karl Marx divided society into two main groups. One group owns the factories, land, and equipment needed to make things. The other group owns little but their own ability to work. The owners make profit by paying workers less than the full value of what those workers produce.

Max Weber added more layers. He argued that economic position, social honor, and political power are three separate things that do not line up in a neat pattern. A university professor might have high status but modest pay. A skilled plumber might earn well but receive less social respect. Class is not just one dimension.

Pierre Bourdieu introduced the idea of cultural capital. This means the knowledge, tastes, manners, and credentials that help people succeed in schools and workplaces. Families pass cultural capital to their children just as they pass money. A child who grows up attending concerts, reading widely, and learning how to speak in formal settings carries those advantages into adult life. This kind of advantage is easy to mistake for personal talent.
beginner.whatItExplains:
Class analysis explains one of the clearest patterns social scientists have found. Countries with greater income inequality also tend to have lower social mobility. This means children in those countries are more likely to end up in the same economic position as their parents.

In Denmark and Sweden, a child born into a poor family has a much better chance of moving up than a child in the United States or the United Kingdom. This is not because children in one country are smarter or harder-working. It reflects differences in how much class position shapes life chances.

Class also helps explain why people from different backgrounds often have genuinely different tastes in food, music, and culture. These differences are not random personal quirks. They reflect what families pass on alongside money: a whole way of engaging with the world that forms early and shapes choices for many years.
beginner.whereItBreaksDown:
Class analysis runs into problems when it tries to explain everything. Not all inequality comes from class. Race, gender, and disability also create serious disadvantages. A Black professional faces barriers that a white manual worker does not. A working-class woman faces constraints that a working-class man does not. Class frameworks that ignore these differences give incomplete answers.

The simple two-group model from Marx also fits modern economies less well than it fit the 1800s. Modern economies have large groups of managers, professionals, and office workers whose position is hard to place in a basic owner-versus-worker split. Many workers now own shares through pension funds. The categories need updating to stay useful.

Class is a powerful tool. But it works best alongside other tools, not as a single answer to all questions about inequality.
beginner.usedAndMisusedC:
Class is used as both a tool for analysis and a weapon in politics. Politicians invoke class solidarity to build coalitions. They also use it to dismiss concerns about race and gender as distractions from economic issues.

The concept gets misused in two directions. When class is used to explain everything, it pushes aside the real disadvantages created by race and gender. When class is dismissed because it cannot explain everything, its genuine power is lost. Most social inequality involves class mixed with other factors. Understanding those combinations is harder than using class as a master key. But it gives a more accurate picture of how society works.

general.theConcept:
A child born into a professional household has roughly a one-in-two chance of entering a professional occupation. A child born into a working-class household has roughly a one-in-six chance. Both children sit through the same school years and take the same exams. That gap does not close. Class analysis was built to explain why it does not.

Class is not just a label for how much money people have. It is a claim about how position is transmitted. Where a family sits in the economic order shapes the schools children attend, the contacts they make, and the confidence they carry into institutions. Different researchers define class in different ways. But they broadly agree that parental position shapes children's chances in ways that go beyond talent and effort alone.
general.problemItSolves:
Equal opportunity in law and equal opportunity in practice are not the same thing. Most wealthy democracies have scrapped legal barriers to advancement and expanded public education. Yet parental income and occupation remain among the stronger predictors of where children land as adults.

If ability and drive fully explained outcomes, then children of professionals would succeed mainly because they worked harder or thought faster. Research shows that parental economic position predicts outcomes even after researchers control for measured ability. Position itself confers advantage. Class analysis tries to name and trace those advantages rather than leaving them invisible behind the language of personal merit.

This matters because policy that ignores the mechanisms will address symptoms rather than causes. Scholarships and test prep can help individual students. They cannot by themselves close a gap that is reproduced across whole social groups.
general.howItWorks:
Pierre Bourdieu, a French sociologist, argued that advantage is stored in three forms of capital. Economic capital is money and assets. Cultural capital covers educational credentials, cultural knowledge, and aesthetic habits. Social capital means networks and relationships. These forms can be converted into one another and are spread unevenly across class positions.

Cultural capital matters especially because it is easy to mistake for individual talent. When universities favor students with broad cultural exposure and confident self-presentation, they partly select for habits that higher-class families have passed on. Bourdieu called these ingrained habits habitus. They shape which opportunities people can see as open to them, not only which ones they can reach.

A second line of analysis, drawn from Max Weber, holds that class, status, and political power are distinct and do not line up neatly. A university lecturer may hold high status with modest income. A prosperous contractor may have the reverse. Keeping these dimensions separate helps clarify what is actually driving outcomes in a given case.
general.whatItExplains:
Research using tax and administrative records has found a consistent pattern. Countries with higher income inequality also show lower rates of movement between income levels across generations. The United States and United Kingdom show much stronger links between parent and child income than Denmark or Sweden. High inequality appears to widen the gap in what parents can invest in schooling and social positioning. That investment then reproduces the original gap in the next generation.

Class analysis also clarifies what some researchers call the meritocracy paradox. Selection processes that place heavy stress on individual merit tend to reward advantages that have been converted into measurable credentials. A student from a higher-class background who attended private school, worked with tutors, and built a portfolio of activities is not only displaying personal capability. Much of what the selection process rewards was built from class resources, not from individual effort alone.
general.whereItBreaksDown:
Class analysis meets its clearest limits when faced with inequalities that do not map onto economic position. Race and gender produce patterns of disadvantage that class frameworks, on their own, tend to underestimate.

Two people in the same occupational class can face very different outcomes if one belongs to a racially marginalized group. Class analysis predicts similar life chances for similar positions. The evidence shows something more complicated. The critique is not that class is irrelevant but that it cannot serve as a full account on its own. Researchers broadly agree that class, race, and gender interact in ways that cannot be reduced to any single factor. How to build that interaction into class frameworks, however, remains a live debate.

The older Marxist picture of two opposing classes also fits modern economies awkwardly. Managers, professionals, small business owners, and workers who hold capital through pension funds all occupy positions that resist simple sorting. Class schemes have grown more detailed in response, which raises a reasonable question about whether the framework is being updated or quietly remade.
general.usedAndMisusedC:
Class analysis tends to go wrong in two ways. One is treating class as the single variable that explains all inequality and recasting concerns about race or gender as distractions from economic fundamentals. Political projects that operate this way tend to underserve the people they claim to represent.

The other failure is reducing class to a matter of tastes, values, and cultural identity rather than ownership and market power. When class becomes a story mainly about who feels looked down on, rather than about who owns what and on what terms people enter work, its analytical edge is lost. Class cultures are real. But they rest on an economic base that a purely cultural reading can obscure.

Class is also invoked to silence other forms of analysis. Appealing to class solidarity to push aside concerns about racial or gender discrimination has a long history in progressive politics, and the pattern has not ended. Used carefully, class analysis is a useful tool for tracing why inequality carries across generations. Used carelessly, it makes some inequalities legible while pushing others out of view.

advanced.theConcept:
The sociological study of class has developed through a sequence of theoretical paradigms, each of which claimed to supersede its predecessor while retaining elements of that predecessor. Marxist class theory established the basic framework of relational class analysis — class position defined by structural relationships to production rather than by individual attributes. Weberian class theory added multidimensionality and marketization. Bourdieuian class theory added cultural reproduction and the concept of habitus. Contemporary class analysis (Goldthorpe, Savage) has moved toward neo-Weberian service class distinctions and cultural approaches that combine sociological with humanistic methods.

The theoretical diversity is not merely academic; it reflects genuine empirical disagreement. The Marxist claim that ownership of productive assets is the fundamental class division is an empirical claim about causal primacy, not just a definitional preference. The Weberian claim that market position, status, and party are three analytically independent dimensions is an empirical claim about their relationship. The Bourdieuian claim that cultural capital is a mechanism of reproduction comparable in importance to economic capital is an empirical claim about inequality processes. These claims are testable, and the evidence bears on them differently.
advanced.problemItSolves:
The most analytically productive recent development in class analysis is the turn toward mechanisms — the attempt to specify the processes through which class position is transmitted across generations rather than merely documenting that transmission occurs. Bourdieu’s habitus concept was a mechanism claim: class position is transmitted through the durable dispositions formed in early socialization. Its weakness is that habitus is difficult to operationalize and measure independently of the outcomes it is supposed to explain.

The mechanism approach has produced more tractable research questions. How do parental education and income affect children’s educational outcomes through specific channels — cognitive development, cultural enrichment, school selection, peer effects, teacher expectations? Each of these is a specific mechanism with identifiable effects that can be measured and compared across contexts. This research has substantially advanced understanding of reproduction mechanisms beyond the broad claims of grand class theory.
advanced.howItWorks:
The empirical measurement of class has been a persistent methodological challenge. Different operationalizations produce different class structures and different findings about mobility. The British occupational class scheme (NS-SEC), Goldthorpe’s Erikson-Goldthorpe-Portocarero (EGP) scheme, and the French Professions et Catégories Socioprofessionnelles (PCS) all measure related but distinct constructs. The choice of measurement scheme is not merely technical; it embeds theoretical assumptions about what class is and how it operates.

The most significant recent methodological development is the use of administrative data — tax records, education records, and labor market records linked across generations — to measure mobility with much greater precision than survey-based methods allow. Chetty and colleagues’ research using U.S. tax data has produced the most precise estimates of intergenerational mobility available, showing both the persistence of class advantage and significant variation across places and birth cohorts.
advanced.whatItExplains:
The class analysis of contemporary capitalism faces a significant challenge: the increasing difficulty of locating individuals in the traditional class structure when employment relations are fragmented, asset ownership is widespread through pension funds, and occupational categories blur. Savage’s Great British Class Survey and subsequent work attempt to map contemporary class structure using a multidimensional framework that includes economic, cultural, and social capital, producing a seven-class scheme that diverges substantially from traditional occupational class schemes.

The empirical findings are provocative: a small ‘elite’ class with high economic, cultural, and social capital, a large ‘precariat’ with low scores on all dimensions, and a more complex middle than traditional frameworks captured. The scheme’s claim to represent contemporary class structure rather than simply an alternative categorization is debated, but its attention to multidimensionality and the specificity of elite reproduction is analytically important.
advanced.whereItBreaksDown:
The most fundamental challenge to class analysis is the relationship between class and agency. If class position shapes preferences, aspirations, and perceived options through habitus, how can individuals engage in the kind of reflexive self-determination that personal responsibility requires? This is the structure-agency problem applied to inequality: if outcomes are determined by structural position, individual responsibility is diminished; if individual responsibility is primary, structural positions cannot explain outcomes.

The resolution most class analysts accept is probabilistic: class position significantly raises or lowers the probability of specific outcomes without determining them. Class is a social-statistical concept, not a deterministic one. But this resolution has implications: if class effects are probabilistic, then within-class variation — the individuals who transcend their class position — requires explanation, and the mechanisms of transcendence may be as important to understand as the mechanisms of reproduction.
advanced.usedAndMisusedC:
The most consequential ideological function of class discourse is the legitimation of existing inequality through meritocratic ideology. If the belief in meritocracy can be maintained — if people believe that outcomes reflect individual merit rather than structural position — then class inequality appears as deserved inequality. The sociological evidence that meritocracy is substantially mythological — that measured ability and effort explain a fraction of actual outcome variance, while structural position explains much of the rest — is politically suppressed in societies with strong meritocratic ideologies.

The inverse ideological function is class fatalism: the use of class analysis to suggest that outcomes are determined by structural position in ways that foreclose the possibility of meaningful individual or collective agency. This misuse is less common in academic sociology but more common in certain political traditions. Both meritocratic ideology and class fatalism distort the probabilistic, contextually variable character of class effects.

foundation.theStory: Social class analysis is the attempt to explain why inequality reproduces itself across generations in societies that claim to offer equal opportunity. The puzzle is the persistence of the correlation between parents’ and children’s outcomes despite formal equality of opportunity. Understanding the competing frameworks — Marxist, Weberian, Bourdieuian — requires understanding what different causal claims they make about the mechanisms of reproduction, not just how they define class.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern which class framework has the most explanatory power (Marxist, Weberian, Bourdieuian, or neo-Weberian), how class intersects with race, gender, and other axes of inequality (the intersectionality debate), and whether contemporary capitalism’s fragmented employment relations and widespread asset ownership require a new class framework. A methodological debate concerns how to operationalize and measure class in ways that do not beg theoretical questions.
foundation.whyItMatters: Class analysis matters because it identifies the structural mechanisms through which inequality persists despite formal equal opportunity. Without understanding those mechanisms, policy interventions aimed at improving social mobility will target symptoms rather than causes. The debate between meritocratic ideology and class analysis is one of the most consequential in contemporary social science because it determines how societies understand and respond to inequality.
questions: Bourdieu argues that cultural capital — tastes, credentials, cultural knowledge — functions as a form of capital that is transmitted within class positions and helps reproduce class advantage. What evidence would establish whether this mechanism is as important as Bourdieu claims — and how would you distinguish cultural capital effects from economic capital effects that operate through cultural channels?

The Great Gatsby curve shows that countries with higher income inequality also show lower social mobility. Does this correlation establish that high inequality causes low mobility, or do both reflect a common underlying cause? What policy implications follow if the causal relationship is genuine?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Marx’s Capital and the Communist Manifesto provide the foundational class analysis. Weber’s Economy and Society provides the multidimensional framework. Bourdieu’s Distinction (1979) and The Logic of Practice (1980) are the essential works on cultural capital and habitus. Goldthorpe’s Social Mobility and Class Structure in Modern Britain (1980) is the foundational neo-Weberian empirical work. Chetty and colleagues’ empirical work on intergenerational mobility using administrative data is the most precise recent empirical contribution. Savage’s Social Class in the 21st Century (2015) provides the most recent attempt to remap contemporary class structure.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Class analysis has the strongest empirical support in two domains: the measurement of intergenerational correlations in income, education, and occupation (where administrative data has produced very precise estimates), and the qualitative documentation of class cultural differences (where ethnographic work has been rich). The weakest area is mechanism specification: demonstrating that specific identified mechanisms account for observed correlations rather than serving as proxies for unmeasured confounds. The habitus concept specifically is difficult to operationalize in ways that allow independent measurement.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about social class must engage with the competing frameworks on their empirical merits rather than simply asserting definitional preference. The most defensible position treats different frameworks as having different domains of application — Marxist analysis for understanding the structural power dimensions of class, Weberian analysis for understanding multidimensional stratification, Bourdieuian analysis for understanding cultural reproduction — rather than as mutually exclusive theories.
questions: Marx argues that ownership of the means of production is the fundamental class division. In contemporary economies where many workers own capital through pension funds and where many professionals have high incomes without owning productive assets, does this remain the most analytically useful class boundary? If not, what should replace it?

The intersectionality critique argues that class, race, and gender are mutually constituting categories whose interaction cannot be reduced to any one of them. Does this critique require abandoning class analysis as a framework, reformulating it to incorporate intersectionality, or treating class, race, and gender as analytically separable dimensions of a multi-factor model?

research.content: Chetty and colleagues’ research using U.S. tax records has produced the most precise estimates of intergenerational income mobility available. Key findings: the probability of reaching the top income quintile for a child born in the bottom quintile is approximately 7.5% in the United States; there is significant geographic variation in mobility within the U.S.; mobility has been declining since the 1980s cohorts. The intergenerational income elasticity — the coefficient relating parent and child income ranks — is approximately 0.45 in the U.S., much higher than in Scandinavian countries (approximately 0.15–0.20). These findings are robust across multiple methodological approaches.

research.topic: The role of social networks in class reproduction
research.content: Recent research has moved beyond income and cultural capital to examine the role of social networks — Bourdieu’s social capital — in class reproduction. Rivera’s Pedigree (2015) and Chetty and colleagues’ Social Capital I and II papers (2022) using Facebook data provide complementary evidence that network connections to higher-income individuals predict economic mobility significantly. The Facebook data shows that ‘economic connectedness’ — the share of high-income individuals among low-income individuals’ friends — is a stronger predictor of upward mobility than school quality or other factors. The mechanisms through which network connections translate into economic outcomes — through information, referrals, norms, or direct resource transfers — are actively researched.

research.topic: The relative contribution of genetic and social class factors to outcome persistence
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: a study that could fully separate the genetic transmission of traits relevant to economic success from the social transmission of class advantage — requiring a design that holds genetics constant while varying class environment or vice versa. Why unavailable: genetic and social inheritance are confounded in natural families (parents transmit both genes and class environment simultaneously), and the designs that would separate them — adoption studies, twin studies — face serious selection and generalizability limitations. What can be established: heritability estimates for economically relevant traits from behavioral genetics studies; correlations between class position and measured cognitive ability. What cannot be established: the precise partitioning of outcome variance between genetic and social class factors in a way that resolves the nature-nurture debate about class persistence.

comparativeNarrative.name: Class as lived experience
comparativeNarrative.content: For people who grew up in working-class households, class is not a theory. It is the daily experience of knowing that certain doors are not for you, that the way you speak marks you out, that schools and offices were built with someone else in mind. Richard Sennett and Jonathan Cobb documented the psychological costs of this in The Hidden Injuries of Class. Diane Reay’s Miseducation traced the same pattern more recently: people who move into higher-status institutions often carry a persistent sense of not quite belonging. What this perspective adds is that class does not only determine income. It shapes how people understand their own worth. When societies say everyone can succeed through effort, those who do not succeed tend to read that as personal failure rather than as evidence of unequal starting points. The damage is quiet and lasting.

comparativeNarrative.name: Human capital versus class
comparativeNarrative.content: Liberal economics mostly resists class as an explanatory category. The preferred concept is human capital: the knowledge, skills, and credentials that people build up through education and training. On this account, income gaps reflect differences in productivity, and parents pass on human capital to their children through education, work habits, and values. The correlation between parents’ and children’s earnings is real, but it runs through choices people make, not through an inherited class position that operates independently of those choices. What this view contributes is a challenge to class analysis on its own terms. If what matters is the transmission of skills and knowledge rather than of a class position, then interventions can focus on improving schools and expanding access to training rather than on changing ownership relations. The debate between the two accounts is partly about evidence and partly about what follows for policy.

comparativeNarrative.name: Class and unpaid labor
comparativeNarrative.content: Traditional class analysis has a blind spot. It focuses on paid work in the labor market and largely ignores the unpaid labor that keeps households running. Cooking, childcare, and care for elderly relatives are not counted as productive activity in most class frameworks. Feminist economists argue this is not a minor oversight. The household runs on unpaid labor, and that labor has historically been done by women. If class is defined by your relationship to paid work, then the women doing unpaid work at home fall outside the frame entirely. What this perspective reveals is that class analysis has tended to picture the typical worker as male. Once unpaid labor is counted, the class map changes. Women’s economic position cannot be read off from the occupation of a male household head, and the mechanisms that reproduce inequality across generations include the domestic labor that traditional frameworks cannot see.

rabbitHole.label: Inequality
rabbitHole.reason: Class analysis is the primary sociological framework for explaining inequality. Understanding class requires understanding the phenomenon it is trying to explain — and the alternative explanations that class analysis competes with.

rabbitHole.label: Karl Marx
rabbitHole.reason: Marx’s analysis of class and exploitation is the founding framework for class analysis. Understanding what Marx actually argued — as opposed to its political appropriations — is the essential starting point.

rabbitHole.label: Capitalism
rabbitHole.reason: Class analysis in its Marxist form is specifically an analysis of capitalist class structure. The relationship between property in productive assets and class power is inseparable from how capitalism operates.

rabbitHole.label: Property
rabbitHole.reason: Property in productive assets is the Marxist definition of the fundamental class divide. Understanding how property rights work is prerequisite to understanding what class analysis claims about the mechanisms of inequality.

rabbitHole.label: Feminism
rabbitHole.reason: Feminist political economy has substantially challenged and transformed class analysis by showing that the exclusion of unpaid care and domestic labor from productive activity is built into the framework’s assumptions.

rabbitHole.label: The Industrial Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Industrial Revolution created the conditions for modern class analysis — concentrated wage labor, urban working classes, and the separation of ownership from labor that Marx analyzed.

rabbitHole.label: Max Weber
rabbitHole.reason: Weber challenged Marxist class analysis by insisting on the independent significance of status and party alongside economic class, producing a three-dimensional stratification framework that remains the standard alternative to pure class analysis in sociology.

rabbitHole.label: The Federal Minimum Wage
rabbitHole.reason: The minimum wage sits at the intersection of class and wages: it directly concerns the floor beneath which market power allows employers to compress working-class compensation, making it a live test of whether law can substitute for the collective bargaining power that union decline eroded.

reference.author: Bourdieu, Pierre
reference.title: Distinction: A Social Critique of the Judgement of Taste (La Distinction)
reference.annotation: The most influential class analysis of the twentieth century. Bourdieu’s account of how cultural tastes, practices, and credentials reproduce class position transformed class analysis from a primarily economic framework to one that encompasses cultural and social dimensions. Essential for understanding how class reproduction operates through the misrecognition of class advantages as individual merit.

reference.author: Goldthorpe, John H.
reference.title: Social Mobility and Class Structure in Modern Britain
reference.annotation: The foundational empirical work of the neo-Weberian tradition. Goldthorpe’s development of the Erikson-Goldthorpe-Portocarero class scheme and his measurement of social mobility across occupational classes established the empirical agenda for class analysis that subsequent work has built on and contested.

reference.author: Chetty, Raj et al.
reference.title: The Fading American Dream: Trends in Absolute Income Mobility Since 1940
reference.annotation: The most precise measurement of intergenerational income mobility available for the United States. Chetty’s use of administrative tax data to measure the probability of children earning more than their parents shows both the persistence of class advantage and its significant geographic variation. The essential empirical reference for mobility claims.

reference.author: Savage, Mike
reference.title: Social Class in the 21st Century
reference.annotation: The most recent attempt to remap contemporary class structure using a multidimensional framework combining economic, cultural, and social capital. Savage’s seven-class scheme diverges substantially from traditional occupational class schemes and captures features of contemporary inequality — particularly the specificity of elite reproduction — that traditional frameworks miss.

reference.author: Wright, Erik Olin
reference.title: Classes
reference.annotation: The essential neo-Marxist class analysis. Wright’s attempt to reconstruct class theory on the basis of multiple asset types — productive assets, organizational assets, and skill assets — bridges the Marxist and Weberian traditions and provides a more nuanced account of middle-class positions than traditional Marxist frameworks.

reference.author: Marx, Karl
reference.title: Capital: A Critique of Political Economy (Das Kapital)
reference.annotation: The foundational text of relational class analysis, defining class by structural relationship to the means of production; the entry treats it as the origin of the Marxist framework whose claim of ownership as the fundamental class division it engages.

reference.author: Marx, Karl, and Friedrich Engels
reference.title: The Communist Manifesto (Manifest der Kommunistischen Partei)
reference.annotation: Cited alongside Capital as the foundational statement of two-class antagonism; the entry repeatedly returns to and critiques this 'older Marxist picture of two opposing classes' as fitting modern economies awkwardly.

reference.author: Weber, Max
reference.title: Economy and Society (Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft)
reference.annotation: The source of the multidimensional framework distinguishing class, status, and party (power); the entry draws its recurring Weberian claim that these three dimensions are analytically independent directly from this work.

reference.author: Bourdieu, Pierre
reference.title: The Logic of Practice (Le Sens pratique)
reference.annotation: Named alongside Distinction as the essential statement of habitus; the entry's account of habitus as a durable, hard-to-operationalize mechanism of class reproduction draws on this work specifically.

reference.author: Rivera, Lauren A.
reference.title: Pedigree: How Elite Students Get Elite Jobs
reference.annotation: Provides the entry's evidence that elite hiring favors upper-class candidates and that social networks reproduce class advantage; cited in the research layer as complementary support for the social-capital mechanism of reproduction.

reference.author: Sennett, Richard, and Jonathan Cobb
reference.title: The Hidden Injuries of Class
reference.annotation: The documented basis for the entry's claim about the psychological costs of class; the working-class-experience narrative draws on this study to argue that class shapes self-worth, not only income.

reference.author: Reay, Diane
reference.title: Miseducation: Inequality, Education and the Working Classes
reference.annotation: Supplies the entry's recent evidence that working-class people in higher-status institutions carry a persistent sense of not belonging, tracing the lived experience of class reproduction in education.

commerce.title: The Sum of Small Things: A Theory of the Aspirational Class
commerce.author: Elizabeth Currid-Halkett
commerce.note: Start here for a readable contemporary account of how the professional class reproduces its position through consumption of time, education, and cultural goods — Bourdieu updated for the twenty-first century, without requiring prior theory.

commerce.title: Pedigree: How Elite Students Get Elite Jobs
commerce.author: Lauren Rivera
commerce.note: A vivid sociological study of how elite firms recruit in ways that systematically favor upper-class candidates under the guise of meritocratic selection — the best entry point for understanding class reproduction in professional hiring.

commerce.title: The Class Ceiling: Why It Pays to Be Privileged
commerce.author: Sam Friedman and Daniel Laurison
commerce.note: Once you have the basic framework, this is the essential next step — Friedman and Laurison show with empirical precision how class background shapes career trajectories even after entry into elite professions.

commerce.title: Distinction
commerce.author: Pierre Bourdieu
commerce.note: The most important class analysis book of the twentieth century — Bourdieu's account of how cultural tastes and practices reproduce class position requires some patience but rewards it with a framework that explains phenomena no other account can.

commerce.title: Social Class in the 21st Century
commerce.author: Mike Savage
commerce.note: The most recent serious attempt to remap contemporary class structure using multidimensional data — Savage's seven-class scheme and his account of elite reproduction are essential for understanding what class analysis looks like after Bourdieu.

popularCulture.title: The Great Gatsby curve
popularCulture.description: Economist Miles Corak named this pattern. Countries with more income inequality also have lower social mobility. Children in unequal countries usually stay in the same class as their parents. The name is a jab. Fitzgerald's novel dreams of rising from nothing, yet the countries most attached to that dream tend to have the weakest record of it actually happening.

popularCulture.title: Parasite — Bong Joon-ho (2019)
popularCulture.description: The film shows a rich Seoul family and a poor one who scheme their way into the rich household as servants. Neither side truly grasps the other's world, and class shapes what each can even imagine, not only what they can buy. The story stays fixed on the trickery and violence between the two families. The larger system that sorted them sits offscreen.

popularCulture.title: The professional-managerial class
popularCulture.description: Barbara and John Ehrenreich coined this term in 1977. It names the salaried professionals who do not own factories but manage and train workers. Some critics argue this group has taken over left-wing politics and shifted attention from wages and ownership to culture and identity. The debate over whether this is a real class or a political story is still active.

popularCulture.title: Succession (HBO, 2018–2023)
popularCulture.description: The show follows a family that owns a global media empire. At the very top, wealth is less about spending than about control: who runs the companies, who speaks in public, who steers politics. The cameras stay inside the family's penthouses and boardrooms. The ordinary people whose jobs and news their decisions reshape almost never appear.

popularCulture.title: Social mobility rhetoric and its limits
popularCulture.description: Every major political party in wealthy countries says it supports social mobility. But talking about mobility means talking about moving individuals up. It avoids the harder question of why some people start so far down. Praising mobility while opposing redistribution lets politicians acknowledge inequality without doing much about the gap itself. Class analysis points to the gap. Mobility talk points to the ladder.

comparativeSummary: Everyone here is looking at the same gap between families at the top and the bottom, but they disagree about what is actually doing the work. People who grew up working-class feel class as identity and self-worth, something stamped on you long before any paycheck. Liberal economists deny class is the cause at all, pointing instead to skills and choices that parents pass down. Feminist economists say the whole frame is built around paid work and quietly erases the unpaid labor at home. So the real argument is not how steep the gap is. It is whether class explains it, and who gets left out when you draw the map.
